text: Upon entering the abdomen, the hernia and its contents are visible. The surgeon begins dissection of the retrorectus space by incising the posterior sheath 10 centimeters below the hernia defect, freeing it from the underlying muscles. The dissection extends to the contralateral side, crossing the midline, exposing the linea alba and bilateral rectus abdominis muscles. The dissection progresses superiorly towards the hernia defect.

The surgeon dissects the hernia contents free from the defect, leaving the hernia sac adherent to the previously placed polypropylene mesh. The rectus sheath is excised from the edges of the fascial defect.

The surgeon dissects the retrorectus space laterally to expose the transversus abdominis muscle, carefully preserving neurovascular bundles. Electrocautery is used to transect the transversus abdominis muscle, revealing the underlying transversalis fascia.

The surgeon carefully dissects the diaphragm free from the transversalis fascia, exposing the superior aspect of the surgical field. The dissection proceeds inferiorly, ensuring preservation of all neurovascular bundles.

Following bilateral transversus abdominis release, the surgeon frees the posterior sheath from the anterior abdominal wall components, revealing the diaphragm. The anterior rectus fascial defect is closed using a two-oh reverse barbed suture in two layers, with visible residual tension, particularly superiorly. The closure incorporates bites of the hernia sac to prevent future seroma formation.

The surgeon closes the posterior rectus sheath in the midline using a two-oh reverse barbed suture, ensuring there is no tension. The remaining space is measured to be eighteen by eighteen centimeters.

A composite mesh, measuring eighteen by eighteen centimeters and containing PTFE and bioabsorbable components, is placed into the retro-rectus space. It is secured to the anterior abdominal wall at cardinal points using three-oh suture. The posterior rectus sheath is then closed transversely with a two-oh reverse barbed suture, completing the procedure.
